questions.questionText: A 65-year-old man presents with sudden onset right-sided weakness 2 hours ago. CT head is normal. MRI DWI shows a bright signal in the left MCA territory. ADC map shows corresponding dark signal. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Acute ischemic stroke (restricted diffusion — DWI bright, ADC dark confirms cytotoxic edema within minutes of onset)
questions.explanation: Core concept: DWI bright + ADC dark = TRUE restricted diffusion = acute ischemic stroke. Why correct: Cytotoxic edema → water shifts intracellularly → restricted diffusion. DWI detects stroke within MINUTES (CT may be normal for 6-12 hours). Why A wrong: TIA resolves within 24 hours WITH normal imaging — DWI positive = completed stroke. Why C wrong: Hemorrhagic stroke = hyperdense on CT; this CT was normal. Why D wrong: Abscess also shows restricted diffusion but has ring enhancement + clinical context of infection. Clinical relevance: CT first to EXCLUDE hemorrhage → then DWI-MRI to confirm ischemia. Thrombolysis window: IV tPA within 4.5 hours. Exam trap: Normal CT does NOT rule out stroke — DWI-MRI is most sensitive early. T2 shine-through can mimic restriction — ALWAYS check ADC. Memory aid: 'DWI bright + ADC dark = Definitely stroke Within hours, Immediate action.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.
questions.conceptTag: DWI acute stroke restricted diffusion ADC

questions.questionText: A patient presents with sudden worst headache of life. Non-contrast CT head shows hyperdense signal in the basal cisterns and Sylvian fissures. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH — blood in basal cisterns, star-of-death pattern, most common cause: ruptured berry aneurysm)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Hyperdense blood in cisterns on NCCT = SAH. Why correct: 'Worst headache of life' (thunderclap) + blood in basal cisterns/Sylvian fissures. NCCT sensitivity ~95% within 6 hours. Star-of-death: blood filling basal cisterns in stellate pattern. Why A wrong: Epidural = biconvex/lenticular, between skull and dura. Why C wrong: Subdural = crescent-shaped, between dura and arachnoid. Why D wrong: Intraparenchymal = within brain substance (hypertension, amyloid angiopathy). Clinical relevance: Most common cause: ruptured berry aneurysm (ACoA most common site). If CT negative → lumbar puncture (xanthochromia). CTA → DSA for aneurysm localization. Exam trap: CT sensitivity decreases with time (95% day 1 → 50% day 5). If clinical suspicion high + CT negative = LP mandatory. Memory aid: 'SAH = Sudden Awful Headache + Star-of-death in cisterns.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.
questions.conceptTag: SAH basal cisterns berry aneurysm CT

questions.questionText: On CT head, an epidural hematoma appears as:
questions.optionB: Biconvex (lenticular/lens-shaped) hyperdense collection that does NOT cross suture lines
questions.explanation: Core concept: Epidural hematoma = biconvex, does NOT cross sutures (dura tightly adherent at suture lines). Why correct: Arterial bleeding (middle meningeal artery in 85%) between skull and dura → biconvex/lentiform collection. Limited by suture lines. 'Lucid interval' — brief improvement then rapid deterioration. Why A wrong: Crescent-shaped = SUBDURAL hematoma (bridging veins, crosses sutures but NOT midline). Why C wrong: Intraventricular hemorrhage — blood within ventricles. Why D wrong: Diffuse edema = loss of gray-white differentiation. Clinical relevance: Temporal bone fracture → MMA tear → EDH. Emergency craniotomy. Exam trap: EDH does NOT cross sutures; SDH does NOT cross MIDLINE (stopped by falx cerebri). Both rules are important differentiators. Memory aid: 'Epidural = Eye-shaped (biconvex), Ear (temporal), does not cross sutEros.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.

questions.questionText: A ring-enhancing lesion on contrast CT in an immunocompromised patient. DWI shows restricted diffusion within the cavity. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Ring enhancement + restricted diffusion in center = brain abscess (viscous pus). Why correct: Brain abscess cavity contains thick pus → restricted diffusion on DWI (bright). This differentiates abscess from tumor (tumor necrosis = NO restriction, T2 dark center). Why A wrong: GBM — ring enhancement but central NECROSIS shows NO restricted diffusion (facilitated diffusion). Why C wrong: Toxoplasmosis — ring-enhancing in basal ganglia in HIV, but NO restricted diffusion typically. Why D wrong: Lymphoma — homogeneous enhancement in immunocompromised; ring in immunocompetent but no restriction. Clinical relevance: MR spectroscopy of abscess: amino acid peak (succinate, acetate, lactate). Exam trap: BOTH abscess and GBM show ring enhancement — DWI differentiates (restriction = abscess; no restriction = tumor). Memory aid: 'Abscess = bright on DWI (thick pus restricts water).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.
questions.conceptTag: Brain abscess ring enhancement DWI restricted

questions.questionText: Multiple periventricular demyelinating plaques perpendicular to the ventricles on MRI, best seen on FLAIR sequence, oriented like 'Dawson's fingers.' Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Dawson's fingers — periventricular plaques oriented perpendicular to ventricles = MS. Why correct: MS plaques are oriented along medullary venules → perpendicular to ventricular surface. FLAIR sequence: best for periventricular lesions (suppresses CSF signal). Dissemination in space AND time (McDonald criteria). Why A wrong: ADEM — large, bilateral, poorly-defined white matter lesions, monophasic, post-infectious. Why C wrong: NCC — ring-enhancing with scolex, not periventricular plaques. Why D wrong: PML — subcortical/juxtacortical, no enhancement, immunocompromised (JC virus). Clinical relevance: MS: ovoid plaques in periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord. Active = enhancing (acute breakdown of BBB). Exam trap: 'Open-ring' enhancement in MS (partial ring) vs 'closed ring' in abscess/tumor. Memory aid: 'MS = Multiple perpendicular Spots (Dawson's fingers) around ventricles.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.

questions.questionText: A child presents with seizures. MRI shows a single cystic lesion with an enhancing mural nodule (scolex) and surrounding edema. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Neurocysticercosis (vesicular stage with scolex — most common parasitic CNS infection, caused by Taenia solium larvae)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Cystic lesion with scolex (bright dot within cyst) = NCC. Why correct: NCC is the most common cause of seizures in children in endemic countries (India). Vesicular stage: cystic lesion with eccentric scolex (mural nodule). Stages: vesicular → colloidal vesicular (edema, enhancement) → granular nodular → calcified nodular. Why A wrong: Brain abscess has ring enhancement with restricted diffusion, no scolex. Why C wrong: Tuberculoma — ring-enhancing solid/caseating lesion, no cyst with scolex. Why D wrong: Pilocytic astrocytoma — cyst with mural nodule but larger, in cerebellum (children). Clinical relevance: Diagnosis: EITB (serology), imaging. Treatment: albendazole + steroids (reduce inflammation). Exam trap: 'Hole-with-a-dot' sign = scolex inside cyst = pathognomonic of NCC. Memory aid: 'NCC = Night-sky with bright sColex dot in Cyst.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.

questions.questionText: A posterior fossa tumor in a child with hydrocephalus. MRI shows a midline vermian mass that enhances homogeneously and shows restricted diffusion. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Midline vermian tumor + restricted diffusion + homogeneous enhancement = medulloblastoma. Why correct: Medulloblastoma — most common malignant brain tumor in children. Midline/vermis, homogeneous, hyperdense on CT, restricted diffusion (hypercellular). Drops to spinal canal via CSF. Why A wrong: Pilocytic astrocytoma — cyst with mural nodule, LATERAL (cerebellar hemisphere), no DWI restriction. Most common pediatric brain tumor overall. Why C wrong: Ependymoma — arises from floor of 4th ventricle, 'toothpaste' extension through foramina of Luschka/Magendie, calcification. Why D wrong: Brainstem glioma — diffuse pontine enlargement. Clinical relevance: Medulloblastoma = PNET. CSF seeding (drop mets). Treatment: surgery + craniospinal radiation + chemo. Exam trap: Pilocytic (lateral, cystic, benign) vs medulloblastoma (midline, solid, malignant) — LOCATION differentiates. Memory aid: 'MedulloBlastoma = Midline, Bright on DWI, Bad prognosis.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.

questions.questionText: CT head in a neonate shows bilateral periventricular calcifications. This finding is most suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Periventricular (subependymal) calcifications = congenital CMV. Why correct: CMV — most common congenital infection. Periventricular calcifications are characteristic. Also: microcephaly, ventriculomegaly, polymicrogyria. Why A wrong: Toxoplasmosis — SCATTERED/DIFFUSE intracranial calcifications (basal ganglia and cortex), not periventricular. Hydrocephalus. Why C wrong: Rubella — vasculitis pattern, basal ganglia calcifications, cardiac defects. Why D wrong: Herpes — temporal lobe involvement (encephalitis), neonatal may show diffuse edema. Clinical relevance: TORCH infections cause congenital calcifications with different patterns. Exam trap: CMV = Periventricular; Toxo = Diffuse/scattered — this is a CLASSIC exam question differentiator. Memory aid: 'CMV = around the Ventricles (periVentricular); Toxo = allover (diffuse).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Neuroradiology.
questions.conceptTag: CMV periventricular calcification vs toxo diffuse
